content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 25, 1977

77-04-235

Mr. Dennis D. O'Neil
City Attorney
City of Newport Beach
3300 Newport Boulevard
Newport Beach, California 92663

Dear Mr. O'Neil:

This is in response to your letter to the Commission
dated April 12, 1977, requesting informal advice concerning
the possibility of a conflict of interest of a certain city
councilman.

The general prohibitions against conflicts of interest
in the Political Reform Act are contained in Government Code
§§87100 and 87103, which provide, in relevant part:

87100. Public Officials; State and Local. No
public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or
in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

87103. Financial Interest. An official has a
financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

.... (c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
promised to the public official within twelve
months prior to the time when the decision is
made; ....

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION 1916 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE LEGAL 1917-5903 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-5623

Dennis D. O'Neil
April 25, 1977                                           Page Two


     I assume from your letter that the Councilman owns a 10
percent interest or greater in his company.  Accordingly, the
Irvine Company is a "source of income" to the Councilman within
the meaning of §87103(c), supra.  Government Code §82030, in
part, provides:

     ... Income of an individual also includes a pro rata
     share of any income of any business entity or trust
     in which the individual or spouse owns, directly,
     indirectly or beneficially, a 10 percent interest
     or greater....


     Since the Irvine Company is a "source of income" to the
Councilman, he may not make, participate in making or other-
wise use his position to influence any decision that "will
foreseeably have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally" on the Irvine Company.
While we are given no specific information with respect to the
amount of money involved in the approval or disapproval of the
subdivision projects, it appears likely that these decisions
will have material financial effect on Irvine Company.  Two
previous decisions of the Commission, No. 75-071, Iris Sankey,
2 FPPC Ops. 157, and No. 76-089, Gary R. Gillmor (in final
draft form), and the Commission's regulations defining
"Material Financial Effect", 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18702, are
enclosed for your information.  Because the decision of the
Council with respect to the Irvine Company is individualized,
it is clear that the effect of the decision on Irvine is
"distinguishable from its effect on the public generally".
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18703.  Accordingly, to the extent
the concept of "materiality" is met, and it appears likely
that it will be, the Councilman in question should refrain
from participating in these decisions.

     Thank you for writing.  I trust this answers your question.
If I can be of further assistance in this or any matter, please
feel free to call on me at 916/322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:glb
enclosures

CITY OF NEWPORT BEACH
OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY
(714) 640-2201

April 12, 1977


Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95814

Attention:  Conflict of Interest Division

Gentlemen:

This letter is in request for an informal written opinion
relative to a possible conflict of interest of a Newport Beach
City Councilman.  The Councilman in question owns a company
which installs and repairs piers and docks for the mooring of
boats.  The Irvine Company, a large landowner in our City, also
operates a number of commercial marinas.  It is estimated that
the Councilman's company receives between $20,000 and $30,000
a year for work done at The Irvine Company marinas.

Perhaps as many as ten or twelve times each year The Irvine Company
is also involved in proposing large subdivision projects with
the City.  At some stage in the review process, the City Council
must act to approve or disapprove of these projects.  Because
of the Councilman's contact with The Irvine Company marinas,
he is concerned that he may have a conflict of interest in
voting on The Irvine Company subdivisions.

Your review of these facts and advice as to whether or not a
conflict of interest exists would be appreciated.  If you require
any additional information or have any questions, please give me
a call and we can discuss the matter further.

Very truly yours,

Dennis D. O'Neil

DENNIS D. O'NEIL
City Attorney

DDO/bc


City Hall • 3300 Newport Boulevard, Newport Beach, California 92663

sections.question: Whether a Newport Beach City Councilman who owns a company that receives between $20,000 and $30,000 annually for pier and deck installation/repair work at The Irvine Company marinas has a conflict of interest in voting on The Irvine Company's subdivision projects that come before the city council for approval or disapproval.
sections.conclusion: Since the Irvine Company is a 'source of income' to the councilman (as the councilman owns a 10% or greater interest in his company which receives income from Irvine), he may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use his official position to influence decisions that will foreseeably have a material financial effect on the Irvine Company. The council's individualized decisions regarding Irvine Company subdivision projects will likely have a material financial effect distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. Therefore, the councilman in question should refrain from participating in these decisions.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a city councilman have a conflict of interest when his company receives substantial annual income from a large landowner whose subdivision projects regularly require city council approval?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The councilman has a conflict of interest and must recuse himself from voting on Irvine Company subdivision projects because these decisions will foreseeably have a material financial effect on his company's income source, in violation of Government Code sections 87100 and 87103.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (April 12, 1977) and the FPPC response letter (April 25, 1977). OCR quality is poor but content is discernible. No formally labeled QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections, but the structure is clear from context. Two enclosures referenced but not included in provided text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a Newport Beach City Councilman who owns a company that receives between $20,000 and $30,000 annually for pier and deck installation/repair work at The Irvine Company marinas has a conflict of interest in voting on The Irvine Company's subdivision projects that come before the city council for approval or disapproval.

CONCLUSION: Since the Irvine Company is a 'source of income' to the councilman (as the councilman owns a 10% or greater interest in his company which receives income from Irvine), he may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use his official position to influence decisions that will foreseeably have a material financial effect on the Irvine Company. The council's individualized decisions regarding Irvine Company subdivision projects will likely have a material financial effect distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. Therefore, the councilman in question should refrain from participating in these decisions.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advised the Newport Beach City Attorney that a city councilman whose company receives $20,000-$30,000 annually servicing Irvine Company marinas has a conflict of interest and must recuse himself from voting on Irvine Company subdivision approvals, as such decisions will materially affect his financial interest.
